Although I am currently enrolled in organic chemistry and the biology research program, SciOly will give me the opportunity to learn more outside of school and to apply it at competitions. 

Secondly, I would like to join the Science Olympiad team because of its community. Additionally, I will be able to meet students from across the region and bond with my own teammates at competitions.

column11: I’d like to join the Bronx Science Science Olympiad team because I really enjoy learning beyond the classroom and applying science to solve real-world problems. I’m excited by the idea of working with teammates who are just as passionate about STEM as I am, and I want to challenge myself in a competitive environment. SciOly gives me the chance to explore new topics in depth, build problem-solving skills, and collaborate with others to push toward a common goal. Being part of the team would let me grow both academically and personally while contributing to the team’s success.
column12:
Designer Genes: I’ve always been fascinated by how DNA carries the blueprint of life, and Designer Genes gives me the chance to dive into genetics and molecular biology more deeply. I’m drawn to the problem-solving aspect of understanding genetic mechanisms and how they influence traits, evolution, and biotechnology.

Codebusters: I enjoy puzzles and logical thinking, and Codebusters combines both in a fast-paced way. I like the challenge of recognizing patterns, applying logic, and staying sharp under time pressure, and I think it’s a great way to strengthen both focus and creativity.

Disease Detectives: Public health and epidemiology interest me because they connect science directly to human lives. I’d like to develop the ability to analyze case studies, identify causes of outbreaks, and think about how diseases spread and can be prevented.

Anatomy & Physiology: The human body is incredibly complex, and I’m curious about how different systems interact to keep us alive and functioning. This event would give me the opportunity to study in detail the structures and mechanisms that make biology come alive in a practical way.

Water Quality: Environmental science feels important and urgent, and I’m especially interested in how water quality affects ecosystems and human health. This event appeals to me because it blends biology, chemistry, and environmental awareness, all while helping me think critically about sustainability.

column12:
1. Experimental Design
I want real hands-on research. This event makes me design, run, and report an experiment on site, which is exactly how I want to grow.
2. Disease Detectives
Public health connects directly to wellness. I like working through data to understand and stop outbreaks in real populations. That kind of problem solving motivates me, especially with all the misinformation and confusion around the topic in recent years.
3. Designer Genes
I love biology and genetics sits at the core of it. Answering questions and analyzing data in classical, evolutionary, and molecular genetics will push me to think like a researcher.
4. Water Quality: Freshwater
Healthy water means healthy people and ecosystems. Learning to evaluate freshwater environments lets me connect biology and chemistry with real impact. There have been many scandals from big corporations regarding water quality that truly disgust me, which motivates me even more.
5. Entomology
I want to broaden my biology beyond humans. Identifying insects and using keys will sharpen my observation and taxonomy skills, which carry over to any lab or field work.
6. Rocks and Minerals
I also want stronger overall STEM experience. Classifying specimens and reading Earth’s processes builds pattern recognition and scientific literacy that round me out.

column12:
1. Disease Detectives: Through my research experiences, I’ve gained exposure to studying specific diseases of interest and exploring ways to prevent their occurrence. I also have a strong background in statistics, which is an essential skill for this event and something I particularly enjoy applying to real-world problems. Last year, I prepared extensively for Disease Detectives and as I tried out for the event twice (beginning of year and middle of the year), which gave me a solid foundation in its content. In addition, I participate in a club where I’ve studied various diseases in depth. Since this event meets on Thursdays, I would be able to fully commit to practices and bring both dedication and prior experience to the team.

2. Entomology: Although insects haven’t been my primary area of interest in the past, I’m genuinely excited by the challenge this event offers. I enjoy memorization and pattern recognition, which I believe are valuable skills for studying the wide variety of insect classifications and overall behaviors. I’m confident that my strong study habits and attention to detail would allow me to quickly learn and retain the material. Beyond that, I see this as a great opportunity to explore a new field of biology, expand my scientific knowledge, and gain experience applying my strengths in a fresh and engaging way.

3. Water Quality: After reviewing past Water Quality exams, I realized that much of the content connects closely with Earth Science, a subject I already have experience and a solid foundation in. I enjoy applying scientific concepts to real-world issues, and water quality is a topic with important public health implications. Especially with my love for diseases, I believe that public health is important and something I would love to learn and prepare for. I believe I could do well in this event and contribute meaningfully to the team.

column11: I’ve always been interested in science, especially biology and physics, and I’d like to explore chemistry further. In middle school I learned some biology, but not as deeply as the topics covered in Science Olympiad. I also took a physics course and loved applying math to everyday life, which made me excited to try a build event. Chemistry has also been interesting to me, and sometimes it is frustrating with all its rules and exceptions, but I’ve found it rewarding once I understand the logic behind it, like in stoichiometry. I think Science Olympiad will enable me to learn more while also challenging me with its competitive aspect, helping me learn to think, react, and perform under pressure.
column12:
1. Designer Genes: I haven’t yet decided what I want to be when I’m older, but I know I want to do something with genetics or the brain. This topic excites me because I love learning how genes act under different circumstances, affect each other, and evolve over time. 

2. Hovercraft: The idea of making something levitate seems remarkable, and I want to try it, especially since I have a basic understanding of physics and kinematics. I’ve solved physics problems on paper, but now I could apply them to real life and create something amazing.

3. Chemistry Lab: I love working in a lab, applying class concepts to experiments, and forming conclusions from evidence. I think there’s no limit to what you can do in a lab, and working with others in a competitive environment seems like a great way to improve my skills. One topic this year is Stoichiometry, my favorite in chemistry.

4. Astronomy: One of my favorite books is The Three Body Problem by Cixin Liu, which helped spark my interest in space. I like to think of the possibilities waiting to be discovered. I also want to learn more about how stars and planets are created, since they contribute so much to our universe.

5. Water Quality: Fresh Water: Marine life interests me because we’ve only explored about 5% of the ocean, which is both terrifying and exciting to me. Water quality plays a huge role in aquatic ecosystems, and I want to learn more about that.

6. Rocks and Minerals: I learned briefly about rocks and minerals in 5th grade but never explored them further. I want to learn more about the different types, their properties, and how they contribute to nature and life.

7. Forensics: Solving mysteries has always been exciting, and when I heard about this event combining that with chemistry, I knew I wanted to try it. Using evidence to prove something for a case seems exciting, and I think working in this environment will deepen my knowledge and sharpen my critical thinking skills.

Being on the team will help me to improve many skills while pursuing subjects I am passionate about. I enjoy learning about science and this team offers the perfect opportunities and resources for me to do so.
column12:
1.  Disease Detectives: 
I am interested in Disease Detectives because it connects science to real world problem solving. I find that combining knowledge on biology and public health to be able to notice patterns to determine a disease and its causes extremely interesting, and this will help to improve my analytical thinking skills.

2. Anatomy & Physiology: Nervous, Sense Organs, Endocrine: 
I would like to join Anatomy & Physiology because I am interested in how the human body works. I am specifically interested in learning more in depth about how the nervous system works and how different organs communicate with each other to regulate these functions. 

3. Designer Genes: 
I would like to join Designer Genes because I really enjoyed learning about DNA and RNA in AP Biology sophomore year and would like to study the genetics on a deeper level. This event would allow me to understand molecular biology, inheritance, and gene expression on a deeper level.

4. Experimental Design: 
I would like to join Experimental Design because I enjoy the process of creating an experiment and the challenge of carrying it out. I have prior experience with conducting experiments though my research work. Additionally, the skills I can develop through this event will help me strengthen my research skills. 

5. Rocks and Minerals: 
I am interested in joining rocks and minerals because I enjoy learning about Earth Science and being able to classify/identify rocks is intriguing to me. This event will help me strengthen my observational skills through hands on testing and reference use. 

6. Remote Sensing: 
I would like to join Remote Sensing because I am interested in how technology is used to study Earth. The event appeals to me because it blends data analysis with environmental science, allowing me to explore topics like climate, weather, and natural processes from a broader perspective.

column11: Joining Science Olympiad is valuable opportunity as it combines academic challenge, teamwork, and hands-on exploration of science. Unlike a typical science class, Scioly allows students to dive deeper into a wide variety of subjects that range from biology to physics, providing a chance to discover new interests and passions. The vents go beyond memorization, it encourages critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity; whether that is through experimentation, labs, exams, or building. Nonetheless, it is team-based competition, allowing students to collaborate, share ideas, and trust one another, skills that are just as important as academic knowledge. Beyond academics, Scioly offers a community filled with motivated like minded peers who support ne another and make the experience fun and memorable. Whether you are aiming for a career in STEM or you simply enjoy challenges and a rigorous environment, Science Olympiad helps you grow both as a student and a person.
column12:
A&P: I want to join Anatomy and Physiology as I enjoy learning how the human works, how each organ work together; depend on each other like everyone who is in Scioly. Not only this,  joining A7P would give me an opportunity to dive deeper into biology, medicine, subjects I am passionate about. Additionally, this event will strengthen my understanding of science, but also provide me a strong foundation in pursuing a career in medicine. 

Disease Detectives: Joining Disease Detectives appeals to me as it would allow me to combine science and real-world problem solving. This event would challenge me to think like an epidemiologist, investigating diseases, how they spread, and how they can be controlled. Furthermore, I also enjoy the idea of using data, logic, and critical thinking to solve complex health cases, which connects to my curiosity in medicine. 

Water Quality: Fresh Water: Water is everywhere, inside us, rivers, lakes. Clean water is essential for life and this event would allow me to explore how water is beneficial to all branches of science and how it protects the environments and ecosystems we live in. But, I also enjoy the idea of learning both about microscopic organisms and larger ecological systems, while also understanding the human impact on natural resources. 

Forensics: Forensics builds the foundation on hands-on science in a more investigative manner. I enjoy solving the daily Wordle, puzzles, and thinking critically. This event would allow me to use chemistry and observation skills. The idea of analyzing evidence and stringing it together, a mystery that is both enjoyable, but meaningful. It goes to show how science is applied in real-life circumstances. 

Experimental Design: Experimental Design allows me to think like a real scientist, but giving me the liberty to be creative while requiring precision, as you will design and carry out experiments under pressure. Moreover, I enjoy problem-solving, collecting data, and interpretation.

column11: I think Science Olympiad has a really strong sense of community that I would really love to be a part of. I've always really loved the sciences, especially learning about them in detail and getting to really know a topic, which is a really big part of Scioly. I know I can learn a lot from my teammates and collaborate with talk with my friends as well as make new ones! Also, looking at the block schedule, all of the events that I'm interested in align really well with all of my other extracurriculars. I didn't know anything about Scioly in freshman year, but in sophomore year, I applied, because I had heard from a few of my friends about how much they liked it. However, I had chosen to apply to too many topics instead of just focusing on a select few that I was really interested in and studied hard for them, which is something that I've learned from and plan on applying to my study plan this year.
column12:
1. Chemistry Lab
I've had a love for chemistry since I first learned about it in 8th grade. In Honors Chem and later AP Chem, we started having more hands-on activities in lab, and I realized that I really enjoyed the experiments alongside . Now that I'm not in Chemistry anymore, I thought that Chem Lab would be an awesome way for me to apply what I've learned over the past two years while also being able to collaborate with other students and compete in this topic. 

2. Rocks and Minerals
I didn't think I would find this subject interesting at first, but after doing a bit of research on each of the topics, rocks and minerals seems like a really complex and diverse topic. I was classifying rocks based on observations the other day with one of my friends as practice, and it was really enjoyable because we were able to have friendly disputes based on our own opinions of each classification and the meanings of each category. 

3. Entomology
Bugs are super interesting, and I feel like we tend to focus on and learn more about larger animals, whether this be in school, museums, or movies. However, bugs play such a huge role in the environment and in our ecosystems, and Scioly is a good way to learn much more about them!

column11:
During my first year on the team, I came to know and admire many interesting, passionate people. Especially during regionals, I recognized and loved the sense of community people ignited, how they uplifted each other’s joy and supported each other’s struggles, and how everyone was celebrated. Additionally, I have the pleasure of working with an intelligent and respectable co-EM whom I’m excited to start teaching Astronomy with.

Last year, I restricted myself to the events I was expected to pursue for my intended field. Now, I’m hopeful to diversify my interests and meet a wider range of scientifically-minded people.
column12:
1. Experimental Design: I am currently taking Sophomore Physical Science Research and want to further explore the thought process and hands-on execution behind an experiment while developing my own skills in those very realms. I'm also drawn to the miscellaneous nature of ED because it emphasizes open-ended creativity without being limited to a single scientific area. Moreover, I'd love to work in a group of three to actively improve my collaboration and communication skills in group settings.

2. Chemistry Lab: As I've been looking for mentors in Research and compiling more materials for our Astronomy event, I've become increasingly interested in astrochemistry and high-energy physics. Additionally, I had a lovely experience with Ms. Zheng as my regents chem teacher the previous year and her teaching inspired me to explore chem beyond the classroom.

3. Disease Detectives: I've been drawn to understanding the patterns between collective populations and individuals, as well as how both are connected on a sociological level. I'm interested in understanding the aspects of this relationship biologically and mathematically. I'm also fascinated by the idea of how peoples' primary defense mechanism is their wit above all physical characteristics: I'd love to learn how technology even managed to evolve alongside people gradually understanding and regulating natural dangers like diseases.

4. Entomology: One of my favorite cartoon characters (named Kinger) had a wife who was an entomologist. While a small detail, my appreciation for the character became an added appreciation for the subject.

On a more specific level, I want to explore the Class and Comp category beyond Astronomy and gain more experience managing pressure with a hands-on physical binder.

5. Rocks and Minerals: See above for the last statement on Ento. Additionally, I have a collection of unidentified geodes and general rocks that I appreciate and would love to familiarize myself with through R&M.

column12:
Anatomy & Physiology (Nervous, Sense Organs, Endocrine) – I lovee learning about the human body, especially the nervous system and hormones. Studying these systems helps me understand how microscopic processes affect overall health, tying directly to my passion for medicine and biology.

Chemistry Lab – Chemistry fascinates me because it explains how matter interacts at a molecular level. Conducting experiments allows me to see theories in action and understand real-world chemical processes.

Disease Detectives – I’m curious about epidemiology and how patterns of disease emerge. This event blends biology, statistics, and critical thinking, which aligns with my interest in understanding health at the population level.

Water Quality: Fresh Water – I care about environmental science and public health. Testing water quality combines lab work with practical impact, showing how science can protect communities.

Forensics – I’m drawn to applying science to solve mysteries. Using analytical thinking to uncover evidence connects my love of biology and chemistry with real-world problem-solving.

Boomilever – I enjoy hands on engineering challenges and problem solving. Designing and building structures that can hold weight while using creativity appeals to my logical and experimental side.

column12:
1) A&P. I did AP Bio last year and it was then that I realized how much interest I had in that subject, but as much as I enjoyed Bio, I was a bit disappointed on how I didn't learn much of the body systems since CollegeBoard took those topics away from the AP exam. So when I heard that A&P was going to do the Nervous, Sense Organs, and Endocrine system, I decided that that event was definitely one of the events I was going to reach for as I'm interested in majoring for Psychology and this year's topics are needed for the field I'm going for.

2) Designer Genes. Similar to the 1st event's reasoning, I'm interested in biology- specifically in the areas of biochemistry/macromolecules, body systems, and genetics. And since this event is about genetics, DOGMA, and more, I'm interested in this event based on my interests in biology I mentioned above. Also, some of the topics in this event, are some that I found easier to understand than some other part of AP Biology.

3) Experimental Design. I originally wasn't too sure if I would enjoy this one, but after some consideration, I decided I wanted to try it out based on my curiosity. This 'academic' event was a standout compared to the other events (besides Chem Lab) due to its hand on and designing experiments aspect. It seems fun to be in this event, which is why I want to try it out for firsthand experience.

4) Codebusters. This event reminds me a lot on escape rooms and puzzles. Whenever I had free time, besides reading or watching videos, I would complete puzzles or riddles I found on the internet. Also, I have experience with this event, and despite the trickiness it has brought me, I would like to try for this event again.

column11: Through Tower last year, I realized how much I enjoyed learning about physics and engineering, and I regretted only joining one event because I loved it and wanted to challenge myself more. I liked SciOly because it gave me more hands on learning time, while also letting me be creative and experimental, and it was amazing being able to compete in the Lexington Invitational as a freshman. I've also really enjoyed contributing to the marketing team, and I want to bring that same dedication to compete academically. Joining would let me collaborate with teammates, build a sense of community, and tackle new challenges while learning and exploring new events.
column12:
1. Boomilever: Last year, I realized I really enjoyed engineering, testing designs and learning to build stable, efficient structures. Boomilever uses the same concepts, like tension and compression, but in a new way.
2. Machines: I got interested in Machines because it uses concepts similar to Tower and Boomilever but teaches me about the academic side more. I think it would help me design better structures, not only for competition but also for real life projects. I’ve always loved testing out mechanical systems—when I was younger, I made pulley systems with a string and bucket on my bunk bed to send notes to my sister on the bottom bunk, pretending we were in a treehouse. It was fun, though not very efficient at the time, and now I’m excited to learn how things really work!
3. Helicopter: When I first saw people testing Helicopters at a meeting, I thought it looked really awesome; when I was younger, I used to love making things like paper airplanes or parachutes to try and get my little lego characters to stay in the air for longer. I think this event would be really interesting and creative in the sense that I can learn more in a hands on way, and experiment to see what works.
4. Experimental Design: I like that Experimental Design is creative, since we get to test ideas on the spot. I think it would help me think faster under pressure, and the concepts we use in this event also show up in chemistry and biology class, so it would strengthen my scientific reasoning outside of SciOly too. 
5. Bungee Drop: I like Bungee Drop because it’s about controlling forces in motion, which feels like a fun twist on what I learned in Tower and Boomilever. I enjoy experimenting to see how small changes affect the outcome, and I think designing a device to safely drop an object will help me apply physics in a hands on way.

column11: I think that joining Science Olympia is an opportunity for me to explore deeper into topics that I’m interested in, but are just briefly mentioned in the curriculum. In addition, I think the Science Olympiad is a great way to meet new people and form friendships with others who share the same interests. Also since events group together students who are passionate about the same topics together, the bonds formed through this shared enthusiasm can be especially strong and lasting.

column11: As I grew up, I watched Harry Potter in class in 3rd grade. It connected me to something: wizardry. As I grew up, I became increasingly interested in Harry Potter, drawing me closer to the world of wizardry. Even then, when I looked at anything; cars, airplanes, phones, spaceships, even plants. I would sit there and ask myself, “How?” I’d look into it and see it as the form of wizardry I was always interested in. I’d spend hours looking into processes, inventions, and see how they work. It was “magic” in my eyes. “How did it work?” “How was it made?” “How did it happen?” The one word that lingered in my mind every single day, even when I lay at midnight staring at the sky: how. As I grew up, I became more and more invested in STEM: to unravel the passion inside me for engineering and creating. To this day, I still wonder what creates. How to create. Why? It unlocks more than just an accomplishment. It builds you, like how you build.

4. Materials Science - I wondered about everything in this world, I want to learn about it.

column11: I want to continue pursuing my passion for science by participating in both old events from last year and venture into new ones I am not familiar with (Dynamic Planet, Rocks & Minerals, Boomilever). While I was mainly active in 2 events last year (Tower & Codebusters), I felt that I was generally too quiet during meetings and missed the opportunity to really engage with the SciOly community. Now that I am also part of the SciOly marketing team this year, I hope that I'm able to experience a new perspective socially, while diving into the science I am familiar with from last year. I also would like to get another chance to be more committed this year in terms of the effort I put in (I didn't qualify for competitions last year) for events.
column12:
1) Codebusters
- This is a returning event from last year I was a part of. I find the material of cracking different cyphers unique from the traditional academic events, which rely more on memorization. I'm familiar with the basic material of this event.

2) Boomilever
- This event basically replaced Tower from last year. Though, I believe the concept and science behind the event is very similar, so I would like to prioritize this Build event.

3) Bungee Drop
- The meetings for a build event (based on last year) are generally more fun compared to an academic event. Bungee Drop is my second choice for a build event, though it's ranked lower than Boomilever due to less familiarity.

4) Dynamic Planet
- I find the ocean/sea an interesting topic, and I would like to learn more about how natural phenomena in water happen. It's a topic I'm unfamiliar with but would be captivated to learn about.

5) Entomology
- In AP Bio, I liked it when my teacher gave us fun facts about the behavior of different organisms, and how that was related to their body structure (for the purpose of teaching evolution). This event explores not only different insects around us, but also external factors such as human impact. The Ecology unit was one of my favorite units from AP Bio.

6) Rocks & Minerals
- While rocks and minerals are touched on very briefly back in middle school science classes, I never had a chance to explore their characteristics deeper, despite wanting to. Through this event, I will have a chance to do so, however relative to the rest of the events, this would be the topic I have a smaller interest in.

column12:
1)Anatomy & Physiology: Nervous, Sense Organs, Endocrine
Anatomy interests me because I want to better understand the systems that control how we move, sense, and react. In archery, I rely on my eyes, muscles, and focus to line up every shot, and I often wonder how the nervous system and sense organs work together to make that possible. I'm also drawn to the endocrine system because hormones affect energy, stress, and even performance. Learning how these systems connect will not only help me in competition but also expand my knowledge of how the human body functions in everyday life.
2)Disease Detectives
Disease Detectives excites me because it combines science with real-world problem-solving. I like the idea of approaching outbreaks like puzzles, looking for patterns and evidence to find the source. It feels meaningful to learn how diseases spread and how scientists stop them, since these skills can apply to global health issues that affect everyone. I also like how the event teaches both analysis and teamwork- skills I want to strengthen as a student.
3)Chemistry Lab
Chemistry Lab interests me because it gives the chance to move beyond memorizing concepts and actually test them in experiments. I like the hands-on aspect of seeing how chemicals react and applying formulas to real problems. It feels rewarding to connect what I learn in class to experiments in the lab. I also want to challenge myself with the problem-solving that comes with chemistry, since it requires patience, accuracy, and creative thinking.

column10: Entomology, Water Quality: Fresh Water, Astronomy, Forensics, Codebusters, Experimental Design
column11: Throughout all of my experiences with STEM subjects, ranging from science courses and research, I’ve often felt overwhelmed by the vast array of topics and unable to find fulfillment, primarily due to the lack of focus on any specific field. However, joining the school’s Science Olympiad provides an opportunity for me to delve deeply into a single subject that truly interests me for once. This would enable me to really dedicate myself to understanding concepts in a niche area, rather than spreading my efforts thin across broader topics. My passion for STEM has always been strong, but the sheer amount of options often left me uncertain about where to start. I feel that, by selecting events in SciOly that genuinely excite me, I can explore my interests more deeply and gain some clarity on what I’d like to do in the future. I’m eager to contribute to the team, especially because I know how strong I am in approaching these sorts of challenges.
column12:
1) Codebusters
This was probably the event that stood out the most for me. I'm a big fan of puzzles, and racking my brain to solve them is fulfilling. Besides sheer interest, I've also been wanting to explore more outside of life sciences, which is what I've been doing for the most part. I find cryptography fascinating, and it's definitely something that I'm eager to learn.
2) Forensics
High schoolers don't really have an opportunity to pursue forensics because of what it entails, but it's such a cool, fascinating, and important field. It’s working with what limited information you have to put a story together, which is something I enjoy a lot! I really do like puzzles, which is what pushed me into choosing forensics.
3) Astronomy
Stars, moons, and other planetary bodies! There's so much that lies beyond our planet, yet the majority of it is incomprehensible. Classification interests me a lot because it seems so simple, yet there are so many components that you have to take into account. I love having a plethora of information available– especially when it's something quite literally out of this world. 
4) Entomology
I’m horrified by insects– which is why entomology interests me! I'm scared of bugs, but they're just tiny creatures and, believe it or not, they have life. Entomology would probably help me (1) get over my fear of bugs, (2) learn more about why they look like that, and (3) figure out which ones are related and how!
5) Experimental Design
Experimental design is essentially the foundation of biological and physical science as we know it. It's so important, yet nobody seems to actually enjoy it, which is sad since it's so practical. I do research, so I feel like I'd not only benefit from this, but I would also be pretty good at it as well!
6) Water Quality
I love applying whatever I learn in real life. Because I work with sustainability, and with so many problems regarding water in our world, I think it's crucial for me to learn more about water quality.

column11: I would like to join the team because I have always been curious about science, chemistry, and engineering. Additionally, I will be able to apply the knowledge I gain through Science Olympiad to my academics, potential future work in the field, and various competitions. column12:
Materials Science - I have always been fascinated with different types of materials, especially nanomaterials. Nanomaterials are extremely durable yet lightweight. If scientists can determine how to mass produce nanomaterials, they could be an invaluable resource in many fields. Material scientists are able to discover novel new materials that can be applied to a variety of circumstances.

Chemistry Lab - Last year, I was in Honors Freshman Chemistry, and I fell in love with chemistry. Chemistry is the study of substances, elements, atoms, and subatomic particles, which make up everything in the universe. Chemical reactions form the basis of many processes around us. In my class, I especially enjoyed the many labs we did, such as the Bunsen Burner lab, the flame spectroscopy lab, and the acid-base titration lab.

Boomilever - I have been interested in building and engineering from a young age. I loved building with LEGOs when I was little, and I created many LEGO skyscrapers and bridges. Now, I build robots out of more complex materials such as aluminum and polycarbonate in my robotics team. I had a great time in my Elements of Engineering class with the bridge project, and my group managed to finish 2nd place out of everyone in the class. I see this build event as the next challenge to further my interest in building and engineering.

How we work and how to keep ourselves healthy is more important than anything else, which is why I am interested in this subject. 5. Dynamic Planet: Oceanography. The Earth is covered of 71% of water. And majority of that is the ocean. That makes me wonder how the ocean is able to sustain so many different varieties of organisms, from the largest animal in the world, to the zooplankton that millions of organism consume.

column11: Many of the events in Science Olympiad capture my interest, and I would like to have a deeper understanding of them. There are some interesting topics I have seen in my favorite movies and TV shows that inspire me to learn more about them as well. I enjoy research, problem solving, and I especially love doing labs. In addition, I categorize myself as more of a STEM person than humanities and would like to focus on science/math aspects as a foundation for any future careers in STEM. column12:
1. Forensics - I absolutely love true crime, murder mysteries, and any type of detective work. My favorite books were the Sherlock Holmes series and the Agatha Christine mysteries. The ways in which people solved crimes interested me a lot, and being a detective was my dream job for a very long time (until I learned about their salary). But I've always wanted to have a better understanding about how science plays an important role in the investigations and be able to do hands on work, such as gel electrophoresis or fingerprint dusting.

2. Disease Detectives - When COVID broke out, I had so many questions. How does it affect the population? Are there any lingering effects after a person recovers? How exactly does the virus affect the body? I've always been interested in how epidemics affect people and how to recognize diseases from symptom, especially because I get sick a lot. And knowledge is one of the best ways of prevention.

3. Codebusters - I enjoy problem solving so much, especially after years on math team. Many of the books I read and movies I've watched involved deciphering codes and riddles, which genuienly seemed so cool to me. Hearing stories about communicating in code during wars was also very fascinating. Learning how to figure out messages hidden in code  builds problem solving skills, which can help me in daily life and future careers.

4. Anatomy & Physiology - Learning biology in eighth grade was very enjoyable for me and I especially liked the human anatomy unit, in which we focused on how body systems interacted. Having knowledge about my own body is not only beneficial for competitions, but can help me understand what goes on inside and make healthier decisions.

5. Dynamic Planet - I absolutely love water: oceans, beaches, swimming, surfing, you name it. I also love underwater ecosystems and am very interested in learning how the ocean works. I also want to see how climate change affects the ocean.

column11: I want to join the Science Olympiad team for a variety of reasons. As a high schooler interested in pursuing a STEM career, particularly Biology or Chemistry, I was drawn to this academic team the most because of its STEM focus. Moreover, I like how multifaceted it is with the wide variety of academic and build events, whether it's working on a team to design and construct a boomilever or applying epidemiology knowledge to a Disease Detectives exam. Furthermore, when learning about each of the events, I found that many of them aligned with my interests and hobbies even outside of school. column12:
Event Rankings: Disease Detectives (1), Experimental Design (2), Anatomy and Physiology (3), Rocks and Minerals (4), Boomilever (5), Materials Science (6)

1) I'm most interested in Disease Detectives as it aligns with my future goals to pursue a medical career. Moreover, my experience in various health related programs have allowed me to gain insight on multiple disease topics as well as develop an interest in epidemiology. I would like to join this event to expand my knowledge of disease outbreaks and epidemiology history and hopefully compete with said knowledge.
2) Experimental Design is ranked second because I am deeply interested in scientific inquiry. (Currently I participate in the Biology Research Program) However, I am especially drawn to the Experimental Design event since it focuses most on the experimenting and analysis aspects of scientific inquiry. 
3) Anatomy and Physiology is my third choice for similar reasons to Disease Detectives but also because I am especially interested in the topics this year.
4) Rocks and Minerals is my next choice because of my experience identifying rocks in earth science. I enjoyed working through the flow chart to determine what rock/mineral something is. 
5) Boomilever is next because honestly, it sounds like the funnest event. Reading through the description, it reminded me of the bridge design/building project I did in engineering class which I loved. In addition, I'd love to be a part of a build event in general for a more hands-on, while still collaborative, experience. 
6) I chose Materials Science because of its strong chemistry foundation. I am currently enrolled in AP Chemistry and I'm interested in all the topics of this event, especially nanomaterials. The only reason this event is lower on my rankings is because I have not taken physics yet.

Being on the team would give me the chance to work with other students who share the same passion for science, and I think collaborating and competing together would make me a stronger learner. I’m especially interested in events related to biology and medicine since they connect to what I hope to study in the future. Joining Science Olympiad feels like the perfect way to grow my problem-solving skills, learn from others, and push myself while doing something I genuinely enjoy as well as being a great opportunity to show colleges my overall growth.
column12:
1. Anatomy and Physiology
I ranked this first because I am deeply interested in biology and medicine. Learning in detail about the human body and its systems directly connects to my long-term goal of studying in the medical field. This event would let me explore beyond what I am learning now and help me find a way to apply my previous knowledge.

2. Disease Detectives
Coming in second, I rank this based on my previously mentioned resounding interest in medicine and biology. As a crucial part of medicine, I am interested in investigating the patterns in how diseases spread and how they can be controlled.

3. Designer Genes
I chose this because genetics is an area of biology I find especially fascinating. Understanding DNA and genetic mechanisms can also supplement my learning in my AP Biology class, helping me strengthen my foundation in science while connecting to my interest in medicine.

4. Water Quality: Fresh Water
I am interested in this event because it combines aspects of biology with earth/environmental science, which is a more diverse approach compared to the rest of the events. Water quality is a necessity for all life on Earth, and many people around the world are struggling to maintain systems that can produce safe water. I am interested in diving deeper into this global issue and applying my knowledge of science to approach it.

5. Chemistry Lab
I see chemistry as a valuable way to build practical and analytical skills needed in medicine and for deeper learning in biology. It would be a challenging but rewarding way to think critically.

6. Astronomy
Although astronomy is less connected to my main interests, I still think it would be fun to learn about space and physics. As a kid, I have always been interested in the worlds beyond Earth and how the universe came from such small, simple compounds. Overall, this event would be a fresh new start from my previous chemistry and biology experiences.

column12:
1. Anatomy & Physiology — I like linking pathways to case scenarios. I study with active recall and can teach teammates weak spots.

2. Experimental Design — I like turning messy questions into clean tests. I control variables, block trials, and analyze fast.

3. Boomilever — Iteration motivates me. I optimize every build, track strength to weight, and adjust joints to lift more with less.

4. Engineering CAD — I like precise models and drawings. I can prep jigs and parts for builds and revise fast after tests.

5. Chemistry Lab — I enjoy careful technique and clear calculations. I can standardize, titrate, and show work that earns rubric points.

6. Machines — Simple, robust mechanisms are fun. I compute MA, torque, and work quickly and check with sanity tests.

7. Bungee Drop — I’m drawn to on the spot calibration. I build stretch curves, fit them to quick math, and hit targets under time so we score reliably.

8. Electric Vehicle — I like systems work that blends wiring, control logic, and testing. I can wire cleanly, tune closed loop speed control, and run disciplined test plans so the car starts, stops, and scores on schedule.

Building light, highly efficient cantilevers sounds very appealing to me. I'm excited to work on designs and experiments with team members to improve our efficiency. 5) Water Quality: Fresh Water. I want to test streams, lakes, and wetlands by performing chemical tests. I would also like to make a salinometer to practice data interpretation. 6) Designer Genes. I would enjoy learning more about all of them.

column10: Boomilever, Water Quality, Designer Genes, Experimental Design
column11: I want to rejoin the Science Olympiad team this year, as I truly find my time on the team to be both rewarding and enjoyable. It’s not just the medals that are keeping me wanting to come back year after year, but the memories I’ve made, whether it be jamming my finger into an inch wide pvc pipe at what seemed like the speed of light, getting a blood blister all while trying to dislodge a ping pong ball from the too-narrow pipe (true story), or just studying for my event, I’ve made so many memories here and can’t wait to make new ones. Also, I believe I would be a valuable addition to the team for the second time. I helped my partners medal in their events, and, when I wasn't knowledgeable enough to help academically, I was consistently there cheering on my teammates. I believe that this attitude towards the team as a whole will help me excel on SciOly.
column12:
1: Water quality - Ever since I heard the age old tale of why NYC pizza tastes good (it's the water!)I have been fascinated by the quality of water. We are so lucky to have good, clean, quality water in NYC, enriched by phytoplanktons and fluorine (these additives to water fascinate me!), which other places do not have. I remember in 7th grade science class (I was also in SciOly at the time, yet this event did not exist, learning about the Flint, MI water crisis. It fascinated me how water quality could be the end of an entire ecosystem- just from minute (or in this case, major) changes in water quality. This topic still fascinates me to this day.
2: Boomilever - In 7th grade, I took on my first event in Science Olympiad- Storm the Castle. I can’t even begin to express the fun I had. Creating a catapult, a trebuchet the next year, all surrounded by friends and watching that tennis ball perfectly land in the 5 gallon bucket was perhaps the pinnacle of my middle school Science Olympiad experience. Now, I want to transfer that love of storm the castle, which transitioned into a love for air trajectory, into my love for the boomilever event. This event combines my love for physics as well as my love to build. I truly couldn’t think of a better event to suit my scientific interests.
3: Designer genes - My family describes me as the “family genealogist,” and, to be honest, I think that description is perfectly apt. I have been truly fascinated by genes ever since the 8th grade living environment, where I often stayed after class to voice my many questions regarding how genes can be modified to fend off disease, express certain traits, and so much more. It enamors me how much potential is stored within our own bodies in the form of miles of DNA. As a sophomore in AP Biology, that love for genealogy is only growing. I plan to use SciOly as a catalyst for my buildup of knowledge and an output for my growing interest.
